_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo/PipeDemo, identyfikatory testowe, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu M2. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant correctly recognizes what a token is in text processing by an LLM (E1).
questions.prompt: In the "LLM Architecture" module, it was shown that the model does not work on words but on tokens. What is a token?

options.text: A fragment of text (part of a word, a character, or a space) into which text is broken down before processing
Always a single whole word separated by spaces
A single page of a document loaded into the model
A model parameter responsible for the quality of the answer

questions.feedbackCorrect: A token is a fragment of text — part of a word, a character, or a space — because the model processes text after breaking it into tokens (tokenization), not by words or pages.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A token is neither a whole word nor a page. Go back to the "Token and tokenization" screen: 1 token ≈ a few characters, and Polish can be "more expensive" in tokens than English.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant explains what the context window is and why a long document may not fit within it (E1).
questions.prompt: What is the context window and what happens when a document, together with the question and the answer, exceeds it?

options.text: It is the maximum number of tokens the model sees at once; once it is exceeded, part of the input must drop out
It is the number of model parameters; once exceeded, the model runs slower but sees the entire document
It is the limit on the number of files you can upload; the text always fits in full
It is the size of the screen on which the model's answer is displayed

questions.feedbackCorrect: The context window is the maximum number of tokens (input + answer) that the model "sees" at once — once the limit is exceeded, part of the input drops out, like sheets of paper pushed off a desk of fixed size.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The context window is counted in tokens, not in files, parameters, or screen pixels. Review the "Context window" screen and the desk analogy.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant applies knowledge of the context window to assess why pasting an entire document does not guarantee that the model has read it (E1).
questions.prompt: A tester pasted an entire 80-page fictional SklepDemo specification into the model and asked a question about a single requirement. The model answered while omitting a key clause. Which explanation is the most accurate?

options.text: The document exceeded the context window, so part of the input (including this requirement) dropped out and the model did not "see" it
The model has too few parameters, which is why it cannot read long documents
Pasting an entire document always guarantees that the model read every page, so this must be a random error
Tokenization removed the spaces, which made the specification unreadable for the model

questions.feedbackCorrect: Correct: "I pasted the whole document" ≠ "the model read it" — when the text exceeds the context window, part of the input drops out and may omit exactly the key requirement.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The cause is neither the parameters nor tokenization of spaces — it is exceeding the context window. Go back to the "Context window" screen and the conclusion that pasting ≠ reading.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies the effects of too small a context when an important fragment drops out of the window (E2).
questions.prompt: Which of the following are direct effects of too small a context window when an important fragment of the input drops out? Select all correct answers.

options.text: Omitted information — the model answers without taking the key requirement into account
Inconsistency — the model "forgets" earlier decisions in a long conversation
Hallucination/guessing — missing data is replaced by fabrication
Automatic retrieval of the missing fragment from the internet to fill in the context
A permanent increase in the model's parameter count to fit the entire document

questions.feedbackCorrect: These are the three effects of too small a context: omitted information, inconsistency, and hallucination. The model does not fetch the missing data on its own, nor does it change its parameter count.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The model does not fill gaps by retrieving from the web or by increasing its parameters. Review the "Three effects of too small a context" screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes QA situations in which too small a context window is a real source of risk to decision quality (E2).
questions.prompt: In which situations from a tester's work is too small a context window a real source of risk to decision quality? Select all correct answers.

options.text: A long conversation about defect ORD-TEST-777 in which the model stops taking into account the decisions from the beginning of the thread
Analysis of an extensive specification where the acceptance criterion is in a section that dropped out of the window
A question about a requirement that the model answers confidently, even though the source data was no longer in the context
A short question about a single definition whose full content fits within the context window
Changing the interface color of the tool in which the model is run

questions.feedbackCorrect: The risk arises wherever an important fragment drops out of the window: inconsistency in a long conversation, an omitted criterion, and a confident but data-unsupported answer. A short question that fits in the window and the UI color are not threats here.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: When the content fits in the window or the change concerns only the UI, the risk of too small a context does not occur. Review the relationship between the context window and its three effects (the "Three effects…" screen).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant arranges in the correct order the steps of text processing by an LLM — from text to answer (E3).
questions.prompt: Arrange in the correct order the steps an LLM performs from receiving text to generating an answer.
sequence: The input text is broken down into tokens (tokenization)
The tokens are placed in the model's context window
The model leverages the relationships between tokens using attention
The model generates the successive tokens of the answer
The answer tokens are assembled back into readable text
questions.feedbackCorrect: Correct: text → tokens → context window → attention on relationships → token generation → assembly into text. This is the intuitive flow "from text to answer".
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Tokenization must come first, then the context window and attention, and finally the generation and assembly of the answer. Review the "Transformer and attention — intuition" screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant orders the processing steps when the input is longer than the context window (E3, E1).
questions.prompt: A tester pastes a long document that exceeds the model's context window. Arrange the steps in the order in which the model processes such an overly long input.
sequence: The model receives the pasted document and the tester's question
The text is broken down into tokens (tokenization)
The number of tokens is compared with the context window limit
Fragments exceeding the limit drop out of the window
The model processes only the tokens that fit within the window
The model generates an answer based on the available context
questions.feedbackCorrect: Correct: receiving the text → tokenization → comparison with the window limit → cutting off the excess → processing what remains → answer. That is why part of the input may drop out before the model even starts computing attention.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Cutting off the excess happens after tokenization and comparison with the window limit, and processing covers only what fit. Review the "Context window" and "Transformer and attention" screens.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant makes a safe QA decision when a document is longer than the model's context window (E1, E2).
questions.prompt: You have to analyze a fictional PipeDemo specification (about 120 pages), and a preliminary check shows that its text significantly exceeds the model's context window. You care about getting a correct answer regarding specific acceptance criteria. What is the best decision?

options.text: Split the document and provide only the sections relevant to the question (e.g., "Acceptance criteria") so that they fit within the context window
Paste everything at once — since the tool accepted the text, the model will surely take every page into account
Paste everything and trust the answer, because a longer input always gives a fuller picture
Ask the model to write in the missing fragments of the specification that it did not fit on its own

questions.feedbackCorrect: When a document exceeds the window, the safe option is to provide only the sections relevant to the question — this way the key criteria really end up in the context and do not drop out.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The tool accepting the text does not mean the model saw all of it; nor are you allowed to ask it to fabricate the missing fragments. Go back to the "Context window" and "Three effects…" screens.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant evaluates a model choice, recognizing that a larger parameter count is not on its own a guarantee of quality (E4).
questions.prompt: A team is choosing a model to analyze a fictional defect ID format in SklepDemo. Model X has significantly more parameters but mistakes the ID format in a test; model Y has fewer parameters but, with well-chosen context, correctly recognizes the format. A colleague proposes: "Let's take X, because it has more billions of parameters". How is it best to evaluate this proposal?

options.text: Reject it as an anti-pattern — accuracy is determined by fit to the task and context; since Y works better on this task, choose Y (or verify both options on real cases)
Accept the proposal — a larger parameter count always means higher answer quality
Accept X, but shorten the context window to even out the models' chances
Choose the model with more parameters and skip the tests, because the outcome is a foregone conclusion

questions.feedbackCorrect: The parameter count is not on its own a guarantee of quality — what matters is fit to the task, data quality, and context. Since Y is accurate on this task, choosing it "because it has more billions of parameters" is an anti-pattern.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: More parameters do not automatically mean better quality, and shortening the context window worsens results. Review the "More parameters ≠ better quality" screen.
